raw.summary: The daf discusses the laws of writing and delivering a get when a husband instructs a group, the distinction between 'write' and 'all of you write', and remedies for various ailments including kurdiyakos (a type of fever).

fields.title: Alternative version of the earlier dispute
fields.summary: Some reverse the opinions of Rav Huna and Rabbi Yochanan regarding whether counting the group is like saying 'all of you'.

fields.title: Baraita on instructions to a group
fields.summary: A baraita teaches that if a husband tells ten people 'write a get and give it to my wife', one writes on behalf of all; if he says 'all of you write', one writes in the presence of all. Similarly for delivery.

fields.title: Dispute: Does counting the group equal 'all of you'?
fields.summary: A dilemma is raised: if the husband counted the ten people, does that have the same effect as saying 'all of you'? Rav Huna says it does not, while Rabbi Yochanan in the name of Rabbi Elazar of Rome says it does.

fields.title: Rav Pappa's resolution
fields.summary: Rav Pappa says they do not disagree: one case is where he counted all of them, the other where he counted only some, and they refer to different sides of the dispute.

fields.title: Rav Yehuda's enactment and Rava's objection
fields.summary: Rav Yehuda instituted a formula for a 'all of you' get: write – either all of you or each one; sign – either all of you or any two; deliver – either all of you or each one. Rava objects that sometimes the husband might cut short and say only 'all of you', leading to invalidation. Rava instead proposes a different formula: write – each one; sign – any two; deliver – each one.

fields.title: Conclusion of the chapter
fields.summary: The mishnah of the chapter is cited: 'He who says...'

fields.title: Mishnah: One seized by kurdiyakos
fields.summary: The mishnah states: If one was seized by kurdiyakos and said 'write a get for my wife', he said nothing. If he said 'write a get' and was then seized and retracted, his latter words are void. If he became mute and nodded in response to questions, we test him three times and if consistent, they write and give.

fields.title: Gemara on kurdiyakos and remedies
fields.summary: The Gemara explains that kurdiyakos is a fever from new wine from the press. It discusses remedies: red meat on coals and diluted wine for sun-related ailments, and various cures including a black chicken, leeks, and immersion. A story is told about Rav Amram the Pious and Yalta's intervention.

fields.title: Stories about Rav Yosef, Rav Sheshet, and the Exilarch
fields.summary: Rav Yosef engaged in grinding, Rav Sheshet in weaving, praising work that warms the body. The Exilarch asks Rav Sheshet why he does not attend his feasts; Rav Sheshet demonstrates that the servants are suspected of serving forbidden meat. A humorous exchange follows with a multi-legged animal and a dish of bread and a jar of wine.
